data.title: SPSM: Small Caps Are Unlikely To Outperform
data.description:
The SPDR Portfolio S&P 600 Small Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:SPSM), launched on 07/08/2013 by State Street Global Advisors, Inc. and managed by SSGA Funds Management, Inc., provides exposure to the small-cap segment of the U.S. equity market by tracking the performance of the S&P SmallCap 600 Index. It charges a 0.03% expense ratio and manages about $12 billion.

Small-cap stocks offer an aggressive approach to investing due to their higher sensitivity to interest rates and credit conditions. And this approach is not warranted right now. Besides the fact that the macro environment suggests challenges for small caps in the near term, large caps are likely to outperform them because of the strength in the tech sector to which they are biased and their more muted response to macro challenges.

Portfolio

The portfolio offered by SPSM certainly has some advantages that I do appreciate in an ETF, the primary being the fewer unpredictable swings its price can have due to the lack of meaningful non-systemic factors. The top 10 holdings are just 5.69% of the fund, and there's none that accounts for more than 0.72%.

But as I explained in the introduction, small caps are more sensitive to interest rate changes than large caps. Besides the fact that they underlie small businesses that don't have the same access to credit that larger ones do, financial institutions represent a larger chunk of this market segment.

Performance

It makes sense that small caps struggle to outperform large caps in the long run. Since SPSM's inception, it has lagged behind them and the return differential only keeps widening.

More recently, its YTD performance has been worse on both an absolute and risk-adjusted basis. SPSM is still in the red, as it didn't recover as much as large caps since the April drawdown.

Outlook

The thing is that this is unlikely to change. Especially now that the market enjoys a break from rapid shifts in trade policy and growth implications.

Speaking of growth, let us begin with understanding the growth narrative to better predict the relative strength or vulnerability of small-cap stocks.

First of all, if you look at the GDP QoQ change, you will notice that there was a 0.5% linked-quarter contraction in the first quarter of 2025. That is significantly different than the 1.5% growth noted by the Final Sales to Domestic Purchasers. That's because the latter excludes net exports, pointing to pre-tariff inventory build-up as the primary cause of the divergence.

The front-loading activity businesses engaged in before tariffs were imposed also removes any positive signal from the uptrend the ISM Manufacturing New Orders Index experienced in April and May. As you can see, the YTD trend points to declining orders for manufactured goods, and the latest data point confirms that aggregate demand is weakening.

Economists forecast slower growth in the second and third quarters and an improving pace afterwards, indicating that the growth challenges will be more modest in the long run, but meaningful in the short term.

On Thursday, we got the latest jobs report from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, which puts the nonfarm payroll YoY growth slightly higher than it was last month. Economists expected 110,000 jobs to be added in June, but the report shows that the U.S. added 147,000 instead. While that was pleasantly surprising, the unemployment rate falling by 10 bps to 4.1% coincides with a 130K drop in the labor force and doesn't necessarily reflect hiring strength; the labor force participation rate has contracted by 0.3% YoY.

Moreover, while the job openings rate is now close to the level at the start of the year, the unemployment insurance claims have meaningfully risen YTD, suggesting a potential higher unemployment in the near term. Besides, businesses are reluctant to hire and hesitant to reduce headcount, as the labor market may be softening but is still tight. As long as economic challenges persist (and they are likely to) and the market keeps cooling, this could change, adding upward pressure to the unemployment rate.

The labor market challenges are followed by inflation ones. While the latest PCE readings show levels close to the Fed's target, we have to merely look at how likely it is that this will persist.

Fed economists have found evidence of a two-month lag between tariff implementation and higher prices. This is supported by the reasonable hypothesis that businesses have to exhaust their pre-tariff inventory before they start passing on the higher costs to consumers in order to preserve margins. So, it's still early to declare victory. Inflation expectations are reflective of this thesis.

The central bank is justified in not cutting further the Fed Funds rate then. The labor market is cooling but still strong enough; meanwhile, inflation data have yet to capture the effects of tariffs and the Fed is most likely to wait longer before reducing the policy interest rate. Be that as it may, FOMC participants forecast two more cuts this year, which indicates that the labor market is going to need support soon and that higher inflation is not going to be a long-term issue.

Fixed-income markets reflect a scenario in which a recession doesn't occur, interest rates go down, and credit markets don't experience any meaningful deterioration. The term spread is higher YTD, and the credit spread has been narrowing again in recent months.

Equity markets are also optimistic. The NAAIM index shows that fund managers remain bullish and the AAII index indicates an increased retail investor exposure level in June, now closer to the one at the start of the year.

So, what we have here is short-term growth and inflation challenges that have yet to surface in the hard data that move markets, paired with now very optimistic market participants. This places all stocks in a fragile position, but small-cap ones lack the strength provided by the AI names that dominate the broader market. Later this year, lower interest rates may change the relative potential, but for now, large-cap funds, although introducing higher idiosyncratic risks, will most likely keep outperforming small-cap ones like SPSM because of the AI narrative and their lower sensitivity to interest-rate change implications.

Your Turn

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Ralph Lauren: Trend Reverting North American EBIT Makes The Stock Attractive
data.description:
After experiencing modest and inconsistent growth over many years, Ralph Lauren Corporation (NYSE:RL) has been showing improving financial results, to the point that in September, the stock finally reached all-time high levels after eleven years.

The most recent quarter represented the last one of its FY 2025, and the results provide investors optimism as all geographical areas experienced great bottom-line momentum, including the long-term struggle in North America. At the same time, the trade agreement between the United States and Vietnam serves as a tailwind for the stock, as it helps calm market uncertainty. Therefore, in this analysis, I will rate the Ralph Lauren stock as a "buy."

Company Description

Ralph Lauren Corporation's flagship brand, Polo Ralph Lauren, is renowned for its versatile style, making it suitable for both everyday occasions and semiformal events, with the iconic polo logo serving as a global reference in American fashion.

In Europe and Asia, which together account for most of the sales, Polo Ralph Lauren is perceived as a higher luxury brand than in the United States, and its products generally have a higher price tag. For example, a classic black polo with a small logo is priced at $100 in the United States, while it costs €125 ($147) in France. At the same time, Polo Ralph Lauren is often more accessible to Americans at larger retailers such as Macy's (M), Nordstrom, and Hudson's Bay, as these retailers tend to offer appealing discounts.

Ralph Lauren Struggling 10-Year View

in billion FY 2025 FY 2015
North America 3.05 4.65
Europe 2.17 1.62
Asia 1.71 0.92

Source: Stock Analysis

Although Ralph Lauren has an excellent brand with strong brand awareness, the company has been experiencing difficulties in its primary market, North America, where growth is lagging. This is the main reason why Ralph Lauren's stock took so long to surpass its highest level. For example, ten years ago, Ralph Lauren achieved $4.65 billion in North American revenues; however, in the most recent fiscal year, the metric declined to $3.05 billion, suggesting long-term challenges within that region.

Nonetheless, what has saved Ralph Lauren over the years is its international sales growth. For example, ten years ago, European revenues were $1.62 billion, and in FY 2025, these expanded to $2.17 billion. Furthermore, Asian sales have also contributed to growth, with revenue of $915.5 million ten years ago rising to $1.71 billion in the most recent quarter.

As a consequence, the revenue mix has shifted away from North America, and it currently represents only 43.1% of the revenues, whereas in FY 2015, North American revenues used to be a third of the total sales. Usually, I would find higher geographical diversification as an appealing characteristic in a company. However, this higher diversification was achieved mainly from a decline in local sales, which doesn't become a characteristic of strength.

FY 2025 Earnings, Outlook & Strategy

FY 2025
Revenue 7.08B
YoY Growth 6.75%
North America 3.05B
YoY Growth 3.38%
Europe 2.17B
YoY Growth 10.51%
Asia 1.71B
YoY Growth 9.12%

Source: Stock Analysis

The fiscal year of Ralph Lauren ends in March and starts in April; therefore, the most recent earnings release covers its most recent full fiscal year. Analyzing the past yearly results, this came as a surprise to the long-term sluggish trend, and again, it is what makes me bullish. In FY 2025, revenue, particularly in Europe, came in strong, and North American sales grew 3.38%. Overall, the total revenue rose 6.75% YoY, which is strong considering that the past five-year CAGR has been much lower at 2.82%.

FY 2025
Operating Income 923.4M
YoY Growth 23.23%
North America 640.1M
YoY Growth 15.63%
Europe 566.2M
YoY Growth 21.82%
Asia 413.2M
YpY Growth 23.01%

Source: Stock Analysis

Although a 6.75% revenue growth could be modest to any apparel company, what the market liked was the significant improvement in the bottom line. All geographical areas experienced significant growth in operating income, with increases above 15%, resulting in a consolidated growth rate of 23.2%. Simultaneously, operating margins expanded for the fourth consecutive year to 13.04%.

Operating Income Growth North America
FY 2025 15.63%
FY 2024 1.05%
FY 2023 -19.73%
FY 2021 -26.75%
FY 2020 -30.47%
FY 2019 -3.22%
FY 2018 1.62%
FY 2017 -29.16%
FY 2016 -20.45%

FY 2022 growth 102.60%

Source: Stock Analysis

To illustrate the importance of FY 2025 in terms of North American operating incomes, excluding FY 2022 as it was the post-pandemic rebound outlier, this marks the first time in many years that EBIT growth has been in double digits, compared to the company's consistently lackluster growth, which resulted in declines in almost every year.

These results, especially within the bottom line, in North America, come as a consequence of the company's plan of reducing underperforming freestanding stores. In FY 2025, the company closed seven stores, ending with 223 in North America compared to 230 in FY 2024. At the same time, this is the third consecutive fiscal year in which Ralph Lauren has reduced its store decrease in North America.

Simultaneously, the company is focusing on making the brand more premium in North America and trying to shift away from more price-sensitive consumers. Because of that, the company exited 60 department stores in FY 2025, and plans to close 90 more in FY 2026, where half of these are expected to come from Hudson's Bay, in which on many occasions the brand could be found cheaper than on Ralph Lauren's DTC channels.

Personally, I am mixed about this strategy of shifting away from wholesale. This was a strategy that cost NIKE (NKE) its market share, and the stock paid its price. Nonetheless, I believe that with Ralph Lauren, the situation could be completely different because, to start, the rationale for this is to expand the brand perception of luxury, and not solely on the idea of increasing gross profits. As mentioned before, the growth of Ralph Lauren over the past ten years has come from Asia and Europe, where the brand has a higher status than in the United States. Therefore, the company is trying to do the same within its local market, although this would be a multi-year strategy to drive its growth and profitability.

Valuation

The financial outlook of Ralph Lauren has been notable, and sell-side analyst recommendations have drastically changed over the years. Three years ago, only 42.1% of the analysts had a buy or strong buy rating on the stock, and currently, with the improving backdrop, that percentage has changed to 79%.

Despite Ralph Lauren's underperformance for many years, its valuation is not the cheapest compared to the 32 companies in the Apparel, Accessories, and Luxury Goods industry. Its EV-to-Sales ratio is 2.41x, almost twice that of the industry, and the EV-to-EBITDA ratio of 14.93% sits 31% higher than the industry average of 11.4x. Nonetheless, the EBITDA margin of 16.15% is higher than the industry median of 11.18%, which might help justify its premium.

Risks

The guidance provided for FY 2026 suggests moderate growth on a constant currency basis. Here, management expects the second half of the year to be challenging in North America due to inflation in freight costs and cotton, as well as the impact of tariffs. Nonetheless, through the years, Ralph Lauren has been strategic in not depending on a single country for its production volume, and no single country represents more than 20% of it. Within this guidance, they expect the American consumer to weaken, and it's a risk to the North American recovery.

Nonetheless, on Wednesday, the United States and Vietnam reached a tariff agreement in which goods imported from Vietnam will pay a tariff of 20%, which is a significant reduction from the proposed reciprocal tariff of 46%. This is a total tailwind as Vietnam is Ralph Lauren's largest sourcing destination, which helps to reduce the uncertainty in its supply chain.

Takeaway

Overall, from a ten-year perspective, Ralph Lauren's financial growth appears weak due to sluggish North American sales. Nonetheless, what happened in the past year, with a stunning operating income growth, is something that makes the stock interesting, as this could be a tipping point, as the brand has been shifting away from wholesale to improve its brand perception and bet more on DTC channels. At the same time, they have a solid balance sheet with an interest coverage ratio of 20.94x, and a return on total capital of 11.12% which is almost twice that of the sector median.

At the same time, the valuation of Ralph Lauren is more expensive than its peer group. However, it has more appealing margins that could justify its price to some extent. Simultaneously, the agreement on tariffs between the United States and Vietnam is a tailwind to the gross profits of Ralph Lauren. Due to the reasons mentioned above, I believe the company is exhibiting great momentum due to its improving financials and outlook in North America, and therefore, I will rate the stock as a "buy."

data.title: Strategy: Unusual Insider Purchases Support Rating Upgrade
data.description:
MSTR stock’s recent insiders’ activities

I last wrote on Strategy Incorporated (NASDAQ:MSTR) stock about 1 year ago in May 2024 with an article titled "MicroStrategy Stock: Sell With The Insiders”. Note that the company has changed its name to Strategy recently. That article focused on the near-term risks I saw at that time, in particular the insiders’ selling activities, and concluded with a sell rating.

Since then, there have been a few material changes for the stock. In the remainder of this article, I will elaborate on the top 2 on my radar: the changes in insider activities and also the new outlook in bitcoin prices. These changes lead me to see an improvement in the return/risk ratio for MSTR and an upgrade rating to hold.

Let me start with the insiders’ latest disclosures. The table below summarizes the insider transactions disclosed in the past 3 months. As you can see, the transactions were still dominated by selling activities, totaling more than $40.3 million. However, a few unusual purchases caught my attention. The most recent insider purchases were disclosed by Patten Jarrod on June 9, 2025. The director of MSTR bought 5,000 of MSTR’s preferred shares at a price of ~$85 per share for a total of $425k. Shao Wei-Ming, an EVP of MSTR, also disclosed a similar purchase with a sizable total of around $300k. Overall, insiders’ purchases in the past 3 months totaled more than $2.3 million.

MSTR stock: insider purchases in focus

The above transactions were unusual in my mind for a couple of reasons. First, as just mentioned, MSTR’s insider transactions used to be completely dominated by sellers as detailed in my last article. Thus, the recent insider purchases could signal of a change of sentiment among the insiders. Second, the insider activities in the overall stock market have also been dominated by insider sales lately. For instance, the next chart displays the net buy/sell transactions in the overall market in the 2 months. As seen, the picture is heavily dominated by sales shown by the red bars. Insider purchases (shown by the green bars) are so dwarfed in comparison.

Although, note that the above insider purchases were for MSTR’s preferred shares. As a background, MSTR recently issued three preferred stocks, a 8% Series A Perpetual Strike Preferred Stock (NASDAQ:STRK), a 10% Series A Perpetual Strike Preferred Stock (NASDAQ:STRF), and a 10% Series A Perpetual Stride Preferred Stock (NASDAQ:STRD). Preferred shares, of course, have different characteristics when compared to the common shares. However, my view is that the preferred and the common shares are based on the same business fundamentals. As such, I consider the purchase of the preferred shares disclosed by the insiders as a reflection of improved business fundamentals for MSTR, as detailed next.

MSTR stock: bitcoin holdings

The preferred shares, together with other sources of funds (to be detailed more later), provide more financial resources for MSTR to continue to acquire Bitcoins (BTC-USD) and also invest in its infrastructure. Of course, such a strategy is sustainable in the long term only if BTC shows sufficient price appreciation. The developments surrounding BTC since my last writing, especially the potential policy changes from the Trump administration, have led me to feel more optimistic about the long-term potential of BTC.

Thanks to the improved prospects of BTC and also the large price rally since my last writing, MSTR’s bitcoin holdings have enjoyed a drastic expansion in valuation. As seen, the company has held Bitcoin since 2020 and continued to add to its position. It currently holds 597,325 BTC with an average cost of around $70,982. With the current BTC price, this results in a significant gain of close to 55%. The current market value of these Bitcoin holdings is $65.64 billion.

Despite this substantial rise in its Bitcoin valuation, MSTR’s current overall market capitalization of $114.4 billion still is at a large premium over its direct Bitcoin holdings and/or its book value. As seen in the chart above, Bitcoin's value accounts for only about 57.4% of its market cap. In terms of book value, the current stock price represents a ratio of more than 3.55x as you can see from the following chart.

Other risks and final thoughts

There are certainly some reasons that could help to justify the above premium over its BTC holdings and/or book value. To start, I consider MSTR a leveraged play on BTC due to its ability to raise capital via debt, the preferred shares mentioned above, and also equity offerings. This ability to raise capital, combined with the company’s expertise to use various strategies, certainly justifies some premium. Secondly, while MSTR is almost synonymous with Bitcoin in many investors’ mind, it is fundamentally an enterprise software company with organic operating income. Its operation segments, even if overshadowed by Bitcoin, still generate revenue and profits and should, of course, add to the company’s valuation.

In terms of downside risks, MSTR's cash flow from operations has seen quite small and very unstable though as you can see from its following cash flow statement. Over the past 2 years, the company reported a positive cash flow for 3 of them. In the past quarters since June 2024, the company has been reporting negative cash from operations of in the range of -$23.3 million to -$2.4 million in the most recent quarters. In the meantime, Capital expenditure has risen substantially. It totaled $1.1 million for the June and Sept quarters of 2024 but rose to $10.8 million in the December 2024 quarter and $8.7 million in the March quarter of 2025.

As a result, the company has been relying heavily on debt financing. As shown in its following balance sheet, MSTR's long-term debt has surged considerably. In the past two years alone, its long-term debt has dramatically increased to the current level of $8,140 million from about $2,182 by the end of 2023. As just mentioned, such a leverage strategy is only sustainable for the long run if BTC prices keep appreciating in my model.

To recap, this reexamination on MSTR (and also its associated preferred shares) is motivated primarily by changes since my last writing: the changes in insider activities and also the new outlook for bitcoin prices. After weighing these changes, I now see a better balance between its upside potential and downside risks, leading to a rating upgrade to hold.

data.title: European Banking Stocks Surge Ahead Of U.S., Asia Rivals In H1 2025
data.description:
European banks' stock performance eclipsed that of their US and Asian counterparts by a wide margin in the first half, marking a turning point for the sector.

Despite the market shock triggered by the US tariffs announcement in April, the S&P Europe BMI Banks index jumped 51.59% over the six-month period, versus a 14.54% rise in the US S&P 500 Bank index and 17.97% in the S&P Pan Asia BMI Banks index, S&P Global Market Intelligence data shows.

European banks have reported higher profits since central banks began hiking interest rates aggressively in 2022 to tame surging inflation. The lending income boost from higher rates allowed banks to deliver bumper capital distributions to shareholders. But despite interest rates falling in 2024, large banks' profits remained strong, backed by revenue growth that was driven by interest rate risk management, stronger fee generation and resilient lending income.

In the last two years, large European banks have outperformed US peers in terms of returns on average equity (ROAE) and average total payout ratio, according to Market Intelligence data. These factors have strengthened the investment case for European banks, analysts said at a June 18 webinar co-hosted by Market Intelligence and the European Banking Federation.

Notable gainers

UK-based Metro Bank Holdings PLC (OTCPK:MTRBF) was among the best-performing European banking stocks in the second quarter with a total return of 48.4%.

Shares in the high street lender started to gain ground in April and spiked on June 14 following reports that it received a takeover approach from private equity firm Pollen Street Capital, which was later rejected by the bank's board following a disagreement on valuation. Its majority owner, Colombian businessman Jaime Gilinski Bacal, was subsequently reported to be considering options for his nearly 53% stake in Metro Bank.

Also on the list is Alpha Bank SA (OTCPK:ALBKF)(OTCPK:ALBKY) with 37.5% in total return. Italy's UniCredit SpA (OTCPK:UNCRY)(OTCPK:UNCFF) recently doubled its stake in the Greek bank to about 20% and said it intends to raise it to as much as 29.9%. Alpha Bank's domestic rival, Eurobank Ergasias Services and Holdings SA (OTCPK:EGFEF)(OTCPK:EGFEY),was also among the best performers with gains of 23.2%.

Another UniCredit target, Commerzbank AG (OTCPK:CRZBF)(OTCPK:CRZBY), joins the list with a total return of 31.0%. The German lender's shares have leaped 77.5% since UniCredit started building up a stake in it in September 2024, and that holding has since grown to just under 30%. In June, CEO Bettina Orlopp said Commerzbank's share price growth reflects its strong performance in recent quarters, in response to UniCredit CEO Andrea Orcel's statement that its stock rally does not exactly reflect its "fundamentals."

Netherlands-based ABN AMRO Bank NV (OTCPK:AAVMY)(OTC:ABMRF), Portugal's Banco Comercial Português SA (OTCPK:BPCGF)(OTCPK:BPCGY) and Spain's Unicaja Banco SA (OTC:UNJCF) also made the top 15.

Worst performers

Six Swiss cantonal banks were on the worst-performing list, including Banque Cantonale Vaudoise (OTC:BQCNF), Graubündner Kantonalbankand Basler Kantonalbank, as authorities hammer out sweeping financial reforms. The country's largest bank, UBS Group AG, is facing a potential extra capital demand of up to $26 billion under the bank reform push.

UK mortgage lender Nationwide Building Society is also on the list with negative total returns of 1.1%, while HSBC Holdings PLC (HSBC) — Europe's biggest bank by assets — logged just 1.8% in total returns.

Other poor performers include Poland's PKO Bank Polski SA (OTCPK:PSZKF) and Alior Bank SA (OTCPK:ALORY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Centrus Energy: Peak Premium, Limited Upside
data.description:
Peak Policy Premium, Waning Fundamentals

We initiate coverage on Centrus Energy Corp. (NYSE:LEU) with a Strong Sell rating and $108 PT. Centrus Energy Corp. provides enriched uranium and technical nuclear fuel solutions for commercial and government utilities and federal agencies globally. The company resides at the global geopolitical center of U.S. nuclear fuel security policy, driven by policy tailwinds, long-term DOE contracts, and a $700 million backlog supporting revenue through FY26E.

However, the market’s paroxysmal enthusiasm for domestic uranium security investments has driven velocity in LEU shares well beyond core operating fundamentals. Despite our above-consensus FY26E $505mn revenue mix backed by straightforward backlog math, accelerated DOE funding, and favorable SWU pricing dynamics, we see the prevailing 5.66x EV/Sales structural overshoot versus our 3.20x EV/Sales justified forward multiple as government contracts offer visibility but deter little incremental upside beyond what is already baked into prevailing price levels. The implied $1.84 billion equity value forecasts 36 percent downside, even factoring in a plausible national security premium.

We call out near-term catalysts ranging from backlog conversion to incremental government commissioning, as well as telegraphed and expressively embedded mechanistically in our already constructive top-line view. Risks around policy inflections, Russia withdrawing from global enrichment, or continued nuclear fuel supply inelasticities deserve respect, but we see investor optimism as long-standing such that it occludes what we assert is a problematic next twelve-month cash burn profile, governance red flags evidenced by recent insider selling, and shares now near historical peak valuation multiples. With risk skewed to the downside and limited asymmetry preserved, we see more compelling R/R opportunities elsewhere in the energy transition complex.

Valuation Disconnected from Reality

We believe Centrus Energy’s market cap reflects an extreme policy premium disconnected from economic fundamentals, creating a broad and acute valuation disconnect. Investors extrapolate early domestic HALEU milestones and lucrative DOE contracts into an unbounded growth trajectory while ignoring hardwired limits on-demand timing, capacity ramp and capital intensity.

At 5.66x EV/Sales, we see a market-assigned forward multiple eclipsing both historical precedents and any peer with comparable business and pipeline opacity. We see a 3.2x forward multiple, justified by empirical conservatism around long-tailed revenue recognition from advanced reactor commercialization and uncertain contract structure and cost sharing from DOE. The bull DCF case for $132 per share is predicated on aggressive utilization assumptions, yet-to-be-awarded public investment and unproven ability to capture 35-50 percent of US HALEU needs by 2035, a case that does not appropriately discount execution and policy risk.

Our downside scenario at empirically grounded multiples derives an equity value of $1.84 billion and 36 percent valuation compression, all while respecting the national security thesis. This mismatch is further amplified by speculative mania emanating from any association with energy transition, with Centrus trading on an outsized premium not reflected in underlying operating leverage or competitive moat. Consensus forecasts embed upside that management itself qualifies as speculative, producing significant downside.

Backlog Conversion Uncertainty

The path from end market backlog to realized revenue at Centrus Energy appears more fraught with structural execution and policy risk than the market assumes. $3.7bn in backlog through 2040 masks both funded contracts and sizable contingent agreements that depend on execution and external capital—specifically Centrus securing billions in public and private investment—a less certain outcome amid competing federal priorities. Of the $2.0bn in contingent LEU sales commitments, only $0.8bn has converted to definitive contracts as of February, with the remainder subject to ongoing negotiation and external financing.

Meanwhile, revenue streams tied to DOE task orders and advanced reactor HALEU demand remain exposed to programmatic delays, appropriations cycles, and shifting energy security priorities, highlighted by the current DOE stop-work on IRA funding pending executive review. Continued Russian supply risk further underscores this fragility, as delivery on existing contracts hinges on annual import waivers and export license renewals—any disruption could trigger delivery shortfalls and strain customer relations and future backlog conversion. We believe the market treats backlog as a given, but the path to realized cash is beset by regulatory, financing, and geopolitical uncertainty. Until Centrus demonstrates secured funding and reliable execution in this dynamic policy environment, we see significant downside in viewing backlog as a dependable forward earnings proxy.

Above-Consensus Growth, Backed by Backlog

We estimate Centrus Energy revenues at $455mn (FY25 estimates, +2.9% y/y) and $505mn (FY26 estimates, +11.0% y/y) versus The Street at $432mn and $464mn, reflecting greater visibility and underappreciated upside from incremental federal contracts. Our view is underpinned by Centrus’ $700mn LEU segment backlog through FY26, which we treat as a high-confidence floor for revenue recognition.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $431.98M 6 $463.91M 10
EBITDA $127.49M 6 $136.92M 10
Net Income $61.93M 6 $68.42M 10
EPS $3.78 3 $3.45 7
SG&A Expenses $49.66M 6 $53.33M 10

We model the full $129mn Department of Energy Phase 2 HALEU contract funding explicitly, adding $60mn to FY25 estimates sales, which appears systematically underweighted on The Street. We also assume multi-year backlog conversion at the 95th percentile of historical run rates and pricing uplift in SWU contracts exceeding 10 percent. Delivery delays of SWUs from the fourth quarter of 2024 into the first quarter of 2025 should further support near-term cadence. Russian supply and government order timing risks remain, but with Department of Energy funding ramping and no near-term import freeze, we view The Street as conservative. Bottom line: above-consensus growth is anchored by backlog floor, contracted federal funding, and disclosed pricing.

Valuation

Our $108 PT is based on a 3.20x forward EV/Sales multiple on our above-consensus FY26E estimate of $505mn, implying an EV of $1.64bn. LEU’s unique position as the sole U.S.-licensed HALEU producer and multi-year contracts with the DOE provide significant backlog and revenue visibility — clear drivers behind a premium to history (target: 3.20x; median: 1.0x) — but we see little support for the nosebleed 5.66x (100th percentile stock; 97th versus peers) given continued governance and fundamental risks.

While our multiple reflects above-market growth (FY26E: +11% year-on-year), margin durability from cost takeout and expected government benefits, it also assumes a steep discount for recent insider selling and limited upside after the widely flagged backlog. Peer multiples remain high (median: 16.4x EV/Sales), yet we view LEU’s discount to the group as appropriately priced given its burn profile and potential policy risks. In sum, our valuation balances LEU’s strategic positioning with a caution against over-exuberance, skewing R/R materially to the downside as fundamentals trail froth.

Risks to Our Strong Sell Rating

A material bullish re-rating could stem from the confluence of US energy policy, contract wins and a front-loaded domestic enrichment build out that meaningfully exceeds our base-case assumptions. Although not our base case, Centrus’s mover advantage and significant federal and commercial positioning—backed by over $3.4 billion in DOE appropriations and several material federal enrichment and HALEU awards—could translate into tasking or funding at a scale and speed not priced into our models if new government initiatives accelerate and/or geopolitical supply disruptions (e.g. a Russian supply ban) prove more material and persistent.

Significant uncertainty remains around the cadence and volume of the LEU/HALEU commercial order book, the execution of large-scale centrifuge manufacturing and the ultimate DOE tasking pace and allocation. These factors could improve cash flow visibility and margin leverage if Centrus captures a disproportionate share of an expanding US enrichment mandate. Likewise, any adverse inflection in the global enrichment market—such as broader Russian sanctions leading to a durable premium price regime for US-only enriched uranium—could drive customers to engage Centrus at higher rates than anticipated and/or generate upside pricing beyond our discount framework. Investors should monitor the cadence and scale of federal contract task orders, any further legislative or executive actions driving emergency US enrichment build-out, and forward indicators of sustained customer willingness to pay a higher national-security premium for Centrus’s production.

Conclusion

We remain firmly at Strong Sell with Centrus Energy as we believe today's market price reflects a maximal policy premium while discounting mounting cash burn issues, governance headwinds, and eroding forward return asymmetry. While our above-consensus growth outlook is underpinned by a deep and visible backlog and significant DOE support, current valuation reflects an extreme, unsustainable disconnect with fundamental drivers and upside that we see as unattainable with limited incremental catalysts ahead. In the meantime, we see capital best allocated elsewhere in the transition universe until sentiment undergoes a reset and risk/reward again rebalances in favor of disciplined entry.

data.title: Goose Mine Could Be Worth More Than The Entire B2Gold! My Only Gold Mining Strong Buy
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

B2Gold (NYSE:BTG) is a Canadian gold miner with significant operations in Mali, The Philippines, Namibia and now Canada, expecting to produce about 1 million ounces of gold in 2025 at an AISC of $1,460 - $1,520/oz (which is ~20% above their levels from previous levels due to projects and issues). Even with a setback at their key mine in Mali back in late 2023 to early 2024, the company still managed to deliver very good numbers and solid production last year. The AISC went up as a result, but that should decrease as their new projects become operational, and CAPEX and production normalize.

The latest data is impacted by very significant CAPEX, as the company is investing massive amounts of money in their projects. Even then, we’re looking at a positive TTM free cash flow, which is definitely impressive and shows just how much potential this company has to produce significant cash flows once the CAPEX normalizes. With normalized CAPEX, I believe there is potential to see B2Gold reach even more than $1 billion in cash flows by early 2030 with gold above $3,000 if their projects turn out as planned, which is very significant on a $4.86 billion market cap, as I’ll mention below.

As I mentioned in the title, this B2Gold is the only gold mining company I’d call a Strong Buy today, thanks to their very significant growth catalysts materializing soon, high potential to grow and expand while mitigating Mali-related risks and the very compelling valuation, especially compared to other companies in the industry - as I’ll talk about below.

Financially, based on B2Gold’s latest earnings report, they are in a very good position, with the current assets above the current liabilities - most of which is in prepaid gold sales - and not a lot of debt. They issued some debt recently, announcing $460 million in convertible notes with a 2.75% interest per year to fund their projects. The equity stands at ~$3.17 billion, up by about $140 million over the last quarter, placing them at a PB ratio of around 1.5, which is not amazing, but still somewhere around the middle of the industry, although their assets are significantly more profitable than others thanks to their low costs. In fact, this is among the best balance sheets we can find in the industry, rivaling even excellent ones like Alamos’ (AGI), although the geography of these assets are very different.

Dividends & Buybacks

To further save some money to develop their projects and improve their short-term flexibility, B2Gold has recently announced cutting its dividend in half, bringing the yield to ~2.2%, which isn’t high, but it’s among the highest in the industry. Still, there is potential to see this brought back once their CAPEX normalizes, although I’d prefer either reinvesting or repaying the debt with it. Or, also smart is what the company actually did, announcing up to 5% in buybacks approved in April 2025 until April 2026! So, that’s actually a good move from the management in my opinion, since the stock trades at a significant discount in my opinion.

Risks & Opportunities

Not only is B2Gold cheap for what they can make today, but they have very significant growth targets of adding 750,000 ounces per year by the end of the decade, being a leader in the industry when it comes to their organic growth pipeline, especially since the industry seems to prefer growing through consolidation (mergers and acquisitions) rather than through developing new projects. Regarding their potential, they actually announced a couple of days ago that the first gold was poured at their Goose mine in Canada. As they promised, commercial production began in Q3 2025 - first pour was on July 1st - and they “continue to estimate 2025 productions at 120-150,000 ounces and average annual gold production for the initial full six years of the project’s operations up to 2031 will total ~300,000 oz/year, based only on existing mineral reserves.” That part at the end is also important, because we’re talking about them expecting to more than double this year’s production by 2031 based only on the existing mineral reserves, meaning that any additional discovery in the project could lead to even better numbers. Still, even without additional discoveries, we are talking about a company that could even produce over $1 billion in cash flows half a decade from now based on their current projects in development, which is very good for a $4.84 billion market cap. That would make them significantly cheaper than their competitors, although they’re already at such a point, trading at very attractive ratios for a while due to the political risk associated with the stock.

As I mentioned, political risk remains probably the main deterrent from seeing this stock at a more rational level given the all-time high levels of gold in times when they are set to significantly improve production. We see Barrick (B) still struggling with Mali after the country announced the change in their mining code back in 2023. This is an example of why some countries are seen as riskier from a political point of view than others, but the difference here is that B2, unlike Barrick, is not really in a bad/uncertain position here. B2Gold and Mali have already agreed on the new terms of the permits, and even got permits for expansion. And a final note here, ANY country could do that, especially with governments changing from left to right or vice versa in significant political shifts almost everywhere on the planet lately. Changes in royalty, taxes, tougher permitting, state ownership, etc. are a risk for any commodity producing company, but I have to say that, for B2Gold, this seems very much overstated, as I mentioned above.

Sure, things can change for the worse - a new regime is what caused this mining code change, by the way - but the company is expanding away from Mali, which should theoretically reduce their perceived risk and attract a higher valuation. For example, low-cost Canadian assets like Alamos’ are traded at P/FCF ratios of ~33.5 today. At worst, B2 could even spin off their non-Malian assets to unlock their significant value, although that was not announced and there's no indication that they plan it, for now.

For a rough "apples for apples" comparison, if we assume a similar AISC, Alamos expects around ~600,000 ounces in production this year and expects it to grow to ~700,000 in 2027, while B2Gold expects its Canadian project to produce around 300,000 ounces during the same time. Alamos trades at a market cap of $11.35 billion today, meaning ~$16,214.28 per gold ounce (production capacity). Get B2’s at the same rate through a spin-off, and we’re talking about a market cap of $4.86 billion, which is almost identical to B2’s current market cap (~$20 million ABOVE today's level) - for one single project, while the rest, meaning the majority of the company, is basically coming for free at this price! Sure, say it’s traded at $10,000 per ounce since Alamos is seen as top quality, and it wouldn’t be fair to assume the same ratios, meaning a Price/FCF ratio of ~20.66, and that’s still a $3 billion market cap, which is ~61.85% of B2’s current market cap - again, for a single project that would barely represent ~20% of their production.

Valuation

As for the valuation, I’ll adjust the starting free cash flow to assume normalized CAPEX of $450 million, which is significantly above the levels they were at before the investment cycle began, but we have to account for higher maintenance CAPEX since they’d have more projects, and also for the fact that last year’s production was lower due to the issues in Mali. As such, I think using an adjusted TTM free cash flow of roughly ~$550 million should give a good idea about what they can produce at current gold prices with CAPEX normalizing in the future, and it might even be on the conservative side.

So, for the DCF valuation of B2Gold, I’ll start with a free cash flow of $550 million and assume a CAGR of approximately 10.5% over the first 5 years, which would imply about 65% total growth over that period - to account for their growth projects in the pipeline and perhaps even some delays - followed by 4% annual growth for the following 5 years as production stabilizes and the company matures. I’ll use a 3% terminal growth rate to reflect long-term gold demand and a WACC of 8% to account for commodity price volatility and geopolitical exposure in its operating regions, which is in line with market estimates (see here and here). With $77.9 million in net debt and approximately 1.32 billion shares outstanding, the resulting DCF model yields an enterprise value of ~$8.3 billion and an equity value of ~$8.22 billion. This implies an intrinsic value per share of approximately $6.21.

That’s ~71.5% above the current price using relatively conservative estimations and including delays in their projects, and there is also potential from a spin-off if the company wants to unlock its real value that way.

By year 10, that's $1.06 billion in cash flows if the assumptions are correct, placing the current P/FCF ratio at less than 4, which is very hard to see since they'd then be able to pay even more than 25% yields (at 100% payout).

Conclusions

Overall, the risk-reward for B2Gold seems too good for me to pass, and I’ve been adding this over time, especially at the beginning of the year. Even though the price increased almost exactly 50% YTD, I still believe B2Gold is one of my Strongest Buys in the gold mining industry, and it’s my only Strong Buy among it. Great balance sheet, huge potential cash flows, deeply undervalued projects, low AISC and a growing diversification of projects remain key advantages for me, but we have to take into account that there’s still political risk that could come both at their current and their in-development mines.

data.title: Helix Energy Solutions: A Multifaceted Offshore Services Powerhouse
data.description:
I am giving Helix Energy Solutions (NYSE:HLX) a buy rating, since the share price is quite below its fair value (liquidation value). Helix Energy Solutions Group is a specialized offshore services company focused on well intervention, subsea robotics, and decommissioning. It serves both oil, gas and renewable energy sectors. Despite a slight revenue decline in this year's first quarter, Helix turned profitable and secured major new contracts, including significant cable burial projects for offshore wind farms in the UK. With its strong liquidity, its balanced capital structure and a niche competitive advantage through cost-effective & adaptive vessels, it is well-positioned for growth amid energy market volatility.

About Helix Energy Solutions

Helix Energy Solutions Group was founded in 1980 in Houston, TX, and has grown to become a leader in providing offshore well intervention, decommissioning and robotics services; especially in subsea operations. Formerly known as Cal Dive International, the company served both the oil & gas and renewable energy sectors, with a fleet of ROVs, specialty vessels and trenching systems.

Helix's operations consist of several activities, including: Well intervention, where semi-subs and riser systems are used for efficient subsea interventions. This is done to maximize the lifespan of all their clients' equipment at sea. In addition, they also deploy ROVs (remotely operated vehicle) and trenching gear for cable inspection, burial and repair. Shallow-water decommissioning, where there is a plug-and-abandonment service, is also used for the clean-up and disposal of the platforms.

Revenue and profitability evolution

In the first quarter of 2024, Helix Energy Solutions recorded a revenue of $296.2 million. This consisted mainly of the three largest segments; Well intervention, robotics and shallow water abandonment. These generated a revenue of $211.3 million, $50.3 million and $26.9 million respectively. The first quarter of 2025 was a bit more difficult, with Helix achieving a revenue of $278 million. We saw a small decrease in the revenue of the well intervention, which fell to $198.4 million. The Shallow water abandonment division also recorded a lower revenue. This amounted to only $16.8 million. The robotics segment was the only one that increased in revenue, although it was only a meager increase to $51 million in revenue.

Helix's profitability also turned a corner; in the first quarter of 2024, a net loss of no less than $26.3 million was recorded, while in 2025 this was a net profit of $3.1 million. This, while the turnover in 2024 was slightly higher than in 2025. This was due to a loss of no less than $20.9 million was recorded in 2024 on convertible bonds. In 2025, this topic was not applicable. The costs classified as 'other expenses' also decreased by $1.9 million. Another major player was the currency exchange rates. In 2024, Helix recorded a currency loss of no less than $2.1 million, while in 2025 this was a profit of $572 thousand.

Key Developments in Recent Months

Royal Win in Hornsea 3 Offshore Wind

In recent months, Helix Robotics solutions (a subdivision of Helix Energy Solutions) has secured a major contract from Seaway7 to bury 500km of inter-array cables for Ørsted’s Hornsea 3 windfarm, which is located approximately 120km off the UK coast. The project is expected to take around 300 days to complete, commencing in Q3 2026. The project is expected to utilise Helix’s Grand Canyon III vessel with T1500 and i Trencher systems.

First Major Deployment of T1400‑2 Trencher

In March 2025, Helix announced the deployment of its newly refurbished T1400-2 trencher, contracted by Prysmian for cable burial in the Mediterranean and North Seas. This six-month project highlights Helix's expanding technical arsenal and geographical reach.

Balance Sheet & Liquidity

As of March 31, Helix Energy Solutions reported a strong balance sheet, notable for its strong liquidity and manageable leverage. Helix had $370 million in cash at the end of March, up slightly from the end of 2024. As we can see; their total debt consists of $9.4 million in current liabilities and $301.7 million in long-term liabilities. The company is leveraged, but not overleveraged. As a result, Helix maintains a balanced capital structure and financial flexibility that can be used to support ongoing projects and potential growth opportunities.

Falling oil prices

A recent decline in oil prices due to geopolitics could have a significant impact on Helix Energy Solutions’ operations, as their customers would generate less revenue, which would also force them to cut costs. This would result in a decrease in demand for Helix’s services in the short term. In the longer term, this could have a positive effect. Because their customers have saved time on maintenance, when oil prices rise, there will be budget available to hire Helix’s services again, and demand will return to previous levels or even higher due to the temporary period in which no maintenance was performed. As stated in Helix’s 2024 10-K filing, the company acknowledges that “volatility in oil and natural gas prices and the cyclical nature of the oil and gas market” are important factors that impact demand for their services and overall financial performance.

Competitive Posture in the Offshore Sector

Helix Energy Solutions operates in the offshore oilfield services sector, where it faces both global and regional rivals. Its two main competitors are Tidewater (TDW) and Oceaneering (OII), which offer subsea services and have larger operations and a broader global reach. Helix itself lags behind in terms of economies of scale. However, Helix has established a distinctive position through its specialisation in subsea well intervention, with a fleet of purpose-built vessels such as the Q4000 and Q5000, which offer a cost-effective alternative to traditional drilling rigs for maintenance and decommissioning tasks on and offshore. Many of Helix’s competitors rely on large drilling rigs to continue intervention, but Helix’s vessel-based approach provides greater operational flexibility and lower costs, particularly in the deeper waters. In addition, Helix’s robotics and decommissioning services position it as a versatile player in the broader energy transition. The specialized focus provides a strategic advantage in securing contracts with major operators such as Shell, Petrobras and Ørsted. According to Helix's 2024 annual report, "their purpose-built well intervention vessels achieve competitive advantages through their lower operating costs, ability to mobilize quickly and maximize their uptime".

Helix Energy Solutions Asset value (fair value)

The way I often use to value a company is equal to the liquidation value of a company. I like to use this formula because it gives me more comfort in my thesis. The growth of a company is not taken into account here, which ensures that you have an extra margin of safety. Cash & ST investments are valued at 100% of their value. As well as the receivables and Net Property, plant & equipment. The inventory is only valued at 40%, since in case of a quick sale only slightly less than half can be obtained for it. The long-term investments and other long-term assets are valued at 80% of their value, and the total of the liabilities is valued at 100% of its value. This ensures that there is an extra margin on it.

The calculator sums up all assets, according to their allocated percentages, and subtracts the total liabilities at the end. A value is then calculated that takes into account both the intangible assets and the intangible assets. I usually focus on the value without the intangible assets, since goodwill etc. can sometimes give a wrong picture of the value of a company in my opinion. The bottom cells in my Excel file are the purchase prices, and these take into account a safety margin of 33%. The difference is simply the difference between the current price and the fair value calculated by the calculator. This currently amounts to only 40.65%.

By filling in the relevant data taken from Seeking Alpha, we see that the 'fair value' should be around $9, and therefore the ideal purchase price would be around $6 (33% discount compared to the fair value). However, since the price is close to the ideal purchase price, it does become interesting to possibly open a position. Please note that this valuation does not take into account the possible growth/stagnation of the company. Nor does it predict how it will perform in the coming year, nor how oil prices will evolve.

We can see down below that HLX's share price has been decreasing in the past 12 months. it is important to remember that the price history is never an indicator of what it will do in the future, but a bit of volatility is necessary to reach our fair value point. A stock without volatility might have a good underlying business, but is not as attractive to value investors.

In Summary

Helix has transformed over the past years from a traditional well interventionist to a diversified offshore services platform. Large-scale trenching contracts and strategic asset deployments are unlocking real revenue growth beyond valuation-driven narratives. While cyclical pressures and execution risk remain, Helix’s strong free cash flow and operational track record position it as a credible player in the energy transition.

data.title: Municipal Midyear Outlook: Hold Fast, Stay Agile
data.description:
By Daryl Clements

In an unsettled climate, municipal investors should have all active tools at the ready.

The municipal bond market faced strong headwinds in the first half of 2025, with tariff and budget policies, including debate over the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBB), weighing heavily on investor sentiment.

It’s understandable that the headlines made investors jittery, but it’s important to look beneath the surface noise at the underlying muni bond market. Based on our assessment, it’s inherently sound and dotted with tax-advantaged opportunities for active, focused and flexible investors.

Weathering a Challenging Environment

Besides the weight of negative policy headlines, the municipal market faced record supply: new issue supply is up 16% year-over-year and 49% over the trailing five-year average. Heavy supply tends to pressure returns, but inflows into muni funds—$17 billion through June 30, 2025—have helped absorb considerable excess inventory.

Supply will likely continue to be the key challenge in 2025 and with average construction costs up 35%, municipalities need to issue more debt to pay for capital improvements. Issuance is also up as more state and local governments reenter the debt market while COVID-19-era stimulus winds down. State and local governments were also pulling forward issuance due to lingering concerns around muni bonds losing their tax-exempt status.

Checking in on the OBBB

Muni bonds may have dodged a bullet relative to the OBBB, considering lawmakers initially had floated the elimination of munis’ tax-exempt status to offset the bill’s other tax cuts. The idea didn’t gain traction and, based on several important reasons, we’re convinced wholesale elimination is on hold indefinitely.

The OBBB may still pose challenges for some munis, particularly in healthcare and higher education. Among other funding cuts, the act could replace a flat 1.4% tax on college and university endowment investment earnings with a more financially burdensome tiered tax of up to 8%. The bill is also targeting Medicaid benefits, including the reduction of caps on provider taxes among states. Lower federal funding of Medicaid in many states could reduce hospital revenues in the end, especially those that rely mostly on Medicaid patients for income. For most larger issuers, however, these challenges will likely be manageable.

Muni Strategies in an Unsettled Environment

Whether OBBB- or excess supply–related, muni issuers are rarely impacted to the same extent, which makes careful credit analysis and selectivity words to live by. And in an unsettled climate like today’s, staying active and nimble should include considering the following strategies:

Own Municipal Credit—Credit, spanning A-rated, mid-grade and high-yield issuers, still offer compelling yields and attractive credit spreads backed by solid issuer fundamentals. In fact, upgrades have outnumbered downgrades for 17 consecutive quarters now. Most municipalities continue to have record-high reserves and layers of budget flexibility to lean on. As we see it, this underpins the muni market’s ability to not only support local communities but to absorb the numerous federal policy changes under consideration.

Extend Duration—With the yield curve currently steeper at longer maturities, we recommend a longer duration, or sensitivity to interest rate changes, relative to the benchmark. This is based on our belief that yields will fall as the economy slows and the Fed eventually will resume its rate-cutting cycle.

Pursue a Barbelled Exposure—Given the steeper yield curve, we think investors should take advantage of the long end but also pair longer maturities with short-term bonds in a “barbell” structure. We think a 15- to 20-year maturity range on the long end is appropriate, as these bonds offer both high yields plus roll—the process of bonds rolling down the yield curve as they approach maturity, which raises their prices when the curve is steep. If the curve flattens, which we expect, it would also lift prices for bond owners. A barbell structure isn’t right for all yield environments, but the ability to take advantage of a changing yield curve shape is one reason why staying active has potential to add value.

We expected some of 2025’s pockets of volatility and appreciate that much of the damage to market returns was contained to the three-day period following tariff announcements. Short-term volatility is still in the cards, but the muni market is resilient and has already rallied from its April lows.

Munis have underperformed this year, but given high yields, a steep yield curve, cheap relative values and supportive technicals, we believe they’re poised to perform well during the second half.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams, and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Upstart: All Eyes On Management Execution In The Storm (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Upstart (NASDAQ:UPST) had looked like it was coming into high gear, but the tariff escalations have cast considerable doubt on the sustainability of that progress. Long-time investors may remember that this stock faced great cyclicality following the pandemic and the rise of interest rates. While management remains confident that their improvements have set the company up to thrive even in future downturns, investors will be looking at how the company performs during these uncertain times. The company has accelerated its top-line growth and management continues to guide for GAAP profitability over the coming quarters. With the stock returning to recent highs, I suggest fading this rally. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

UPST Stock Price

I last covered UPST in March, where I upgraded the stock to a buy rating as I became a believer in the turnaround. The stock has risen 50% since then. That belief is facing a test as the risks of a recession have risen due in part to tariff uncertainty.

I am surprised to see UPST trading near recent highs, even as uncertainty has increased dramatically.

UPST Stock Key Metrics

UPST is a financial tech company which has utilized artificial intelligence to lower its loan origination platform. The success of its models has enabled it to automatically approve an increasing percentage of applications over time. The goal is to achieve a product that can approve a wider range of applicants at comparable or higher rates of return, enabling bank customers to invest more capital than would have otherwise been possible through traditional credit models.

The latest quarter saw the company continue its strong recovery, with originations growing 89% YoY to $2.1 billion.

The company has increased its disclosures to break out the credit profile of its borrowers - we can see below that its recent growth has not come at the expense of its “super prime” originations.

The company delivered 67% YoY revenue growth to $213 million, crushing guidance of $200 million. I note that investors likely still view the stock as having a great deal of cyclically, making the sizable beat less significant.

Contribution margins contracted to 55%, below guidance of 57%. While management noted that this reflects faster growth, I note that this is a potential negative point given that it was just in the prior quarter that management was confidently explaining expectations for contribution margins to remain resilient.

Adjusted EBITDA of $43 million crushed guidance of $20 million (but I again emphasize that investors are unlikely to care so much about the magnitude of these beats, given the presumed cyclicality).

UPST continued to make progress on GAAP profitability and was within striking distance of breakeven.

The company ended the quarter with $600 million of cash versus $1.4 billion of debt. It also carried $815 million of loans on the balance sheet, though the amount not classified as R&D has been declining. I note that it isn’t immediately clear what are the criteria for what is considered an R&D loan and what is not - we are merely taking management’s word here.

Looking ahead, management guided for the company to generate 76% YoY revenue growth to $225 million. Management also slightly increased full-year revenue expectations and reiterated expectations to generate positive GAAP net income for the full year. I wouldn’t be surprised if the company continues to “beat and raise,” but again, the challenge here centers around convincing investors that the growth is “different this time” and will not disappear like it did in 2021 and 2022.

On the conference call, management noted that their “ability to adapt to changing macroeconomic conditions is miles better than it was just a couple of years ago.” However, without any way to really quantify that besides back-tested results, I suspect that such commentary will not win over many investors. Management discussed their new one-year agreement with Walmart’s (WMT) OnePay which makes their lending products available to Walmart customers. Management noted that WMT does have “some share” in the deal but that they “feel very good about the economics and the agreement.” My guess is that investors should lean on the side of conservatism, as improving name brand recognition might end up being the most significant outcome from this partnership, but, of course, we can hope for more.

At the company’s AI Day, management, among other things, touted their improving ability to fully automate loan approvals, which has helped increase their conversion rates.

They notably did not give any financial guidance, which is arguably prudent given their recent track record. I note that at a subsequent investment conference, management may have inadvertently given some rough expectations with their long-term profit expectations, with CEO Girouard discussing a future where the company was generating “$10 billion in revenue and $2.5 billion in EBITDA.” Ironically, this might be viewed as somewhat of a disappointment to some investors given that it seems to imply that the company would be operating at only a 25% EBITDA margin even after a 10x gain in revenues, but again I note that this was not explicitly mentioned as a formal financial target.

Is UPST Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, UPST was trading hands at just around 7x sales.

Following the aforementioned commentary regarding profitability, I am adjusting my long-term net margin assumption to 22%, down from 35%. That places the current valuation at around 31x earnings. I see the valuation settling at around 12x to 15x earnings once the company matures, assuming it is still required to occasionally hold loans on its balance sheet. With the company looking poised to sustain around 20% top-line growth for many years as it takes market share, the stock may eventually grow into its valuation, but it may take as many as 4 years. With the macro environment looking rather uncertain, I do not find it appropriate for the stock to be pricing in so many years of growth.

UPST Stock Risks

UPST is highly exposed to market volatility. While it is very-well possible that the company has fully perfected its algorithms such that loan default rates would not be materially impacted by a weakening economy, I do not yet have the confidence to make such an assertion given that it was less than a handful of years ago that the company had to re-work its algorithms. If the company can successfully navigate through a downturn, then it might be able to earn some credibility in the form of a re-rating. I, however, expect this to take quite some time and with perhaps several cycles required, as it is admittedly difficult to believe that this company would be able to significantly revamp such a long-standing industry. This might cause the stock to continually trade at low valuations, which might hurt near term returns given the not-so-cheap earnings multiple. Management will need to execute on driving both top and bottom-line growth and sustaining the progress - all not such easy tasks for this young company.

UPST Stock Conclusion

UPST is a difficult name to stand by in the current environment, but the company appears to be benefiting from strong fundamental momentum. The next many quarters will show if the company has truly made progress in improving its credit models, and successful execution may help the company sustain this high valuation multiple over the long term. The stock appears to trade at a noted premium to many fintech peers, perhaps due to having a popular retail investor presence, but the elevated macro uncertainty calls that premium into question. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

data.title: What Amazon Needs To Break Out
data.description:
Amazon.com (NASDAQ:AMZN) has a love for round figures. After stabilizing through the pandemic, Amazon traded between $150 to $200 till 2022, fluctuating between the support and resistance. Through the rate cut pressures of 2022-23, the trading range became $100 to $150. Almost a buy around $100 and exit around $150 trade. In the mega cap rally of 2023 to 2024, the trading range shifted up to $150 to $200. Since then, it is hovering around the $200 mark, with a potentially shifted trading range between $200 and $250 now.

The predictable and slow-moving nature of Amazon made a buy below $200 during the recent tariff sell-off a no-brainer. I had recommended a Buy at around $167 (another roundish figure two-thirds of the way down in the $150 to $200 range). That call, in hindsight, was in a zone when buying anything would have been a great move. Of course, for a defensive stock, Amazon corrects more generously than rallies from breakout points (not rebounds - that's usually strong). So, buying Amazon on crashes is easier than many other names, even among the defensive group. Combining that with strong fundamentals, it is far easier to bet on Amazon than anything else when the markets are crashing. It feels safe and comfortable at a time when catching other falling knives becomes arduous. Amazon, after all, has not seen the explosion many other big tech names have in the past. A generous correction is often the zone for Amazon - a relatively comfortable buy with cushioned downsides backed by fundamentals that have not truly reflected in prices at a time when many other big tech names have soared.

The graphs below show how Amazon has not been able to break away even amongst the defensive tech names since the pandemic. Also, a graph showing how a blind pick from the defensive techs would have done during the tariff lows. Amazon has not outperformed other defensive tech plays significantly in both the short-term and long-term timeframes.

No wonder, my Buy call has turned out successful. A 31% return in 2–3 months is just about the best Amazon can do going by its price action history. With macro pressures in play and an optimal reward reaped in, should investors book profits? My recommendation was a fundamental long-term Buy, but what do fundamentals say now (with emphasis on why Amazon doesn't break out meaningfully and often plays by trading ranges)? More importantly, perhaps what can propel Amazon to the next trading range of $250-$300? These are some questions I will evaluate in this analysis that will help take a call on what to do with Amazon now.

Why Was I Bullish Amazon?

A lot of factors I was bullish about should be seen from the lens of what prices Amazon was trading at back then. However, revenue growth in Q1 2025 has only improved from when I went long. Even taking FX drags into considerations, the net sales show a 9% YoY jump. Operating income has jumped 20% YoY to $18.4b. Net income per share has seen a robust increase too, from $0.98 to 1.59.

A lot of these improvements suggest structural tailwinds that should support Amazon fundamentally going ahead, not a quarter of execution excellence alone. AWS sales grew 17% to $29.3b, with operating income climbing ~23% to $11.5b. Some of the margin improvement is likely due to workforce optimization and balancing data center expansion rates. But there are structural levers supporting AWS margins too. Use of Trainium and Graviton chips could lower compute cost per unit going forward as well. A strategic shift to longer-term contracts should also help capacity planning. Most importantly, I will watch the continued topline growth in AWS, which potentially points to revival of cloud demand and stronger AI-driven surge. I expect this tailwind to continue to expand faster than expected as more foundational model hosting, training and inference workloads increase. Management did talk about the Tranium2 and the Bedrock Model developments that will support multi-billion AI revenue streams.

Improvements in Amazon's headline numbers were also helped by the e-commerce resilience in the face of tariff pressures. However, this is an area that could fluctuate. Temporary stocking up before tariff regimes settle could be moving the margins and topline here. I see Amazon making the right moves to brace for any tariff impact that are structurally supportive. Such as regional fulfillment centers that could cut shipping costs and times and cushion any international shipment drags. The move to private label products is already ongoing to reduce reliance on Chinese imports. And of course, like most other trade impacted supply chains, restructuring supply chain towards India, Vietnam and Mexico should also reduce impacts. Then there is AI-driven forecasting to reduce tariff impacts through operational discipline and expansion of its logistics moat outside internal use (increasing efficiencies of scale in addition to expanding revenue streams). Given all the support, tariffs will still be a drag and the e-commerce leg will have to rely on executional and innovative plays to keep the segment performing well and then grow.

Ad revenues are the fastest growing segment and expected to hold healthy margins too. Q1 2025 saw a continuation of the trend with a 19% YoY growth in topline. This stream will likely see benefits from AI tool rollouts that can auto-generate creatives and widen the ad buyer base. Amazon Primes' ad rollouts have started to sink into the topline as well, and upcoming quarters should see the impact of a stronger ad uptake there. Amazon's ad outlets are far more diverse anyway than Meta or Google's with Search, DSP, Video and Fire TV giving Amazon a launchpad capable of being resilient to ad spending cuts.

Overall, Q1 2025 trends do show structural supports. Management guidance is not too pessimistic, with Q2 sales expected to land between $159b-$164b. Management did highlight caution around tariffs and their ripple effects. Free cash flow is already under pressure from heavy capex spending around logistics and AI. Not a problem per se, given the reinvestments are in high-growth areas. But capex and cost discipline could be watched more closely now. From what I was expecting from Amazon in April 2025, I think overall there are no major red flags in the long-term story. In fact, strides made since support development is core pillars of my thesis. I am enthused and confident about AWS growth in the mid-teens and a growing Ad revenue. However, retail may need to optimize margins as pressure from tariffs will weigh on the e-commerce arm. Long-term capability build-outs in AI, logistics, rural delivery and Project Kuiper suggest steady expansion of the Amazon ecosystem and moat. But short-term pressures from competition (cloud), tariff escalations and a macro slowdown could keep the lid on Amazon shares till the dust settles.

What Needs to Happen for Amazon

For Amazon to move into the next trading range of $250-$300, a lot of further developments need to show up still. And, of course, near term drags should stabilize. AWS is definitely at the core of the thesis. Stronger enterprise adoption of AI/cloud tools showing further structural gains after the recent rebound is a key re-rating trigger. More AI native services and a demonstration of pricing power in the face of competition will be another key area to watch out for. A growth level of 20-25% in AWS and if sustained could push Amazon beyond the $250 barrier now. As of the next few quarters, that is not expected.

What will also help is AI and productivity payoffs becoming more tangible. Custom chips and Bedrock investments are starting to trickle into real revenues. But markets will like to see how AI monetization is shaping in the cloud, and also in logistics and ads segments. Some public use cases and stories like Microsoft's Copilot will help. This story will likely again need a few quarters to play out.

The current uncertainty of retail will also need to improve. Higher ad margins and product targeting, retail dominance without pricing pressure and new monetization avenues will be keenly watched. Again, this is not likely to show strength in the near term. The near term is more about how well Amazon is able to defend the business in the face of slowing macro and tariff drags.

Till these measurable outcomes show up, markets will likely watch out for capex control and FCF growth. What does not boost Amazon's chances of tipping over $250 is the current macro backdrop of higher rates for longer, consumer spending slowdown and consolidation of tech valuations after a run-up.

Amazon's valuations at ~14x EV to forward EBITDA is not expensive, but a far cry from the ~10-11x around where I had initiated a Buy call. I don't see immediate support for a re-rating towards the 15x plus zone, which is historically the high zone for Amazon. Re-ratings can occur only when multiple levers fire in tandem, i.e. AWS growth accelerating toward 20%+, margin expansion in retail and meaningful AI monetization. Without that trifecta, a move beyond 15x EV/EBITDA may be difficult to sustain. This supports the idea that Amazon could consolidate between $200 and $250 in the near term. Price targets beyond that range will need either earnings beats or a shift in macro sentiment. Until then, the current valuation reinforces my view that booking profits after a 30%+ move is prudent, and re-entering below $200 offers a better risk-reward.

Waiting on Amazon A Prudent Option

After analyzing all the competing forces, it is clear that Amazon is not plagued by any long-term fundamentals scare. However, the short term does not look conducive for further upside at this point. Those who were able to enter at $167 may want to book profits, looking at the immediate lack of triggers for an upside. $250 is likely to be a good resistance. On the other hand, I don't expect Amazon to break the $200 support anytime soon as well.

Amazon is a Hold for the near term. Any decline towards or below $200 could be a re-entry point. Or some of the re-rating triggers discussed kicking in. Long-term Amazon investors have nothing to fear, but should be prepared for a few quarters of consolidation. Any crash on Amazon is its strength territory. It may sound a given for all stocks, but Amazon's crashes have historically seen the fastest and strongest rebounds, making it a most comfortable "buy on crash" stock. For now, book profits and wait. Or wait if you are a long-term investor, not affected by a year's consolidation.
